audit_items.proof_trace: In the SASS_LIST and SASS_MAP cases, `c2ast()` allocates `l`/`m` as raw pointers (refcount=0, no RAII wrapper) before entering a recursive loop that calls `c2ast()` on nested elements. If any nested element has tag SASS_ERROR or SASS_WARNING, the recursive call invokes `error()` which throws `Exception::InvalidSyntax` (error_handling.cpp:230). On unwind, the raw `l`/`m` pointer has no destructor to call, and the allocated List/Map object (plus any already-appended children) is permanently leaked. The `eval.cpp` caller checks only the top-level return tag for SASS_ERROR/WARNING before dispatching to `c2ast()`; nested error elements in lists/maps are not filtered.
audit_items.attack_scenario: A C-registered Sass function returns a SASS_LIST containing a SASS_ERROR element; when libsass evaluates a Sass file that calls this function, `c2ast()` allocates the List object, begins populating it, then throws on the nested error element, leaking the partially-constructed List and all appended children. Repeated invocation (e.g., a Sass loop calling the function) causes unbounded memory growth.
